message.abstract:
<jats:title>Summary</jats:title>
                <jats:p>On Monday, March 16, 2020, the government of Colombia announced actions to control COVID-19. These recommendations directly affected the entire population and included: reducing physical contact; reducing mobility and cancelling unnecessary travel; working from home; cancelling mass events; a 14 day at-home isolation period for people who arrived from international trips, or in cases in which someone in the household experienced a high temperature and sustained coughing; as well as increasing the frequency of hand washing and the use of face masks on public transport.</jats:p>
                <jats:p>In order to understand the public sentiment around these recommendations, Asociación Profamilia developed an online survey through SurveyMonkey®. The survey was completed by 3549 adult people in Colombia (+18 years) between April 8 and April 20, 2020. In this report, we present the results of the survey:</jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>98% think that the COVID-19 pandemic is a serious problem in Colombia.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>90% are concerned that someone in their family will get COVID-19.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>88% are concerned that someone in their family may have an emergency and not receive medical care.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>46% believe they will likely get infected under the current Colombian government measures.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>92% of adults reported taking at least one of the following measures to protect themselves from the COVID-19 infection:</jats:p>
                      <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>85% of senior citizens (60 years and older) voluntarily isolated or complied with government’s mandatory isolation measures.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>82% cut down on their mobility (avoided going out, using public transportation, and traveling).</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>79% of those showing symptoms voluntarily isolated themselves and complied with government isolation measures.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>78% increased the frequency in which they use disinfectants, alcohol, and sanitizing gel, and how often they wash their hands.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>73% refrained from going out to social events and crowded places.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>70% avoided kissing and shaking hands.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>69% immediately complied with the government’s mandatory preventive isolation measures.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>63% avoided using public transportation.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>46% went into self-isolation (voluntary quarantine) before the government decreed it.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>32% started working from home.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                      </jats:list>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This study reveals that there are at least three groups of people in the country who are responding to the pandemic and physical distancing measures in different forms: those who resist the situation (34%), those who suffer from it (26%), and those who accept it (40%).</jats:p>
                      <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>In the group of people who are resisting 40% are men; 68% are under 39 years old; 40% have savings and one out ten reported mental health problems; and 64% supported the government measures.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>In the group of people suffering from the pandemic, 73% are women, 64% are under 29 years old, 55% have an average family income over 2 million pesos COP (roughly 417 GBP), 61% have had some chronic disease or somebody in the family; 73% reported mental problems. This group had the higher support and adherence to the government measures (68%).</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>In the group of people who are adapting to the situation, 76% are women, 43% are over 49 years old, 36% have savings, and 63% have not had chronic illnesses and 73% reported mental problems. This group had the lower support and adherence to the government measures (63%).</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                      </jats:list>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>69% complied with government’s mandatory preventive isolation measures. This percentage was lower (64%) among young adults (25-29 years old).</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Overall, 83% are complying with preventive isolation and physical distancing. 77% feel that complying with isolation contributes to stopping COVID-19.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Hygiene practices such as hand washing (78%), avoiding kissing and hand-shaking (70%), as well as using face masks (69%) were perceived as more effective measures to prevent the spread of the virus compared to physical distancing measures like cancelling travelling (46%), avoiding contact with people with fever or respiratory symptoms (43%), and avoiding contact with people who have travelled in the last month (35%).</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>80% live in the five cities with the highest spread of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                      <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>50% are responsible for the care of a family member; 16% are women heads of household.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>68% mentioned do not have savings.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                        <jats:list-item>
                          <jats:p>25% did not work before COVID-19.</jats:p>
                        </jats:list-item>
                      </jats:list>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The top three concerns among the respondents were that someone in their family may get infected with COVID-19 (79%), that someone in their family may have an emergency and not receive medical care. (74%) and the fate of the poorest and most vulnerable people (71%).</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>During quarantine, 75% have experienced issues with their mental health: 54% felt nervous; 52% have felt tired for no reason; 46% felt restless and impatient, and 34% felt anger and rage; 20% have experienced a need for sexual and reproductive health in the last 21 days which has not been met. 17% are concerned about their children’s bad behaviour and domestic violence.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>63% get informed about COVID-19 through official websites; 52% have found false information about COVID-19 and the pandemic. 40% have experienced or witnessed jokes about the spread of COVID-19, and 25% have experienced or witnessed discrimination against health care personnel.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>38% believe that the National Government’s response to control the virus was clear and consistent and 29% believe that it acted in a timely and swift manner; 46% believe that the Local Government’s response was clear and consistent and 42% believe that it acted in a quick and timely manner.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:p>
title: Social response to early-stage government control measures of COVID-19 in Colombia: population survey, April 8-20 2020

Christina Atchison , Leigh Bowman , Jeffrey W Eaton et al. Public Response to UK Government Recommendations on COVID-19: Population Survey, 17–18 March 2020. Imperial College London (20-03-2020), doi: https://doi.org/10.25561/77581.

Kwok KO , Li KK , Chan HH et al. Community responses during the early phase of the COVID-19 epidemic in Hong Kong: risk perception, information exposure and preventive measures medRxiv 2020.02.26.20028217
Duffy B , Allington D. The Accepting, the Suffering and the Resisting?: the different reactions to life under lockdown. King’s Coll London. 2020;(April).
